web.title: Point-of-Care Ultrasound in the Emergency Department - PMC - NIH
web.description: In this study, ED physicians, PAs, NPs, and RNs were surveyed to assess their opinions and beliefs on the perceived benefits and barriers to the use of POCUS.

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC11163417  PMID: [38859928](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38859928/) ## Abstract ### Background Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) is a valuable tool that assists in diagnosis and management of patients in the emergency department (ED) while being cost-efficient and without the use of ionizing radiation. To discern the opinions and perceptions of ED staff about POCUS applications and barriers, we conducted a cross-sectional survey of employees of 12 EDs in North Texas. ### Methods Participants completed a 20-item online survey about POCUS with questions pertaining to four domains: (1) employee and training information, (2) perceived benefits, (3) common applications, and (4) barriers to use. Out of 805 eligible ED employees, 103 completed the survey (16.1% response rate). ### Results The results indicated a generally positive perception of POCUS among all employee types. Physician had significant exposure and training of POCUS than non-physician group ( _p_ < 0.001). Physicians tend to find cardiac assessments more useful for clinical management than non-physicians (47% vs. 23%, _p_ = 0.01), while non-physicians find soft tissue/abscess assessments more useful (27% vs. 9%, _p_ = 0.01). ### Conclusion The most significant barriers to POCUS use were time constraints for physicians and a lack of training for non-physician employees. Our study provides valuable insights into the perceptions of multiple ED professionals, serving as a foundation for promoting POCUS use in the ED. **Keywords:** emergency department , point-of-care ultrasound , survey ## Introduction Point-of-care ultrasonography (POCUS) is a useful Emergency Department (ED) tool with rapidly expanding clinical applications around the world. [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-1) - [9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-9) Compared to traditional consultative ultrasound or other imaging modalities, such as X-ray or computerized tomography (CT), POCUS image acquisition and interpretation are performed in real-time at the patient’s bedside. There are many potential advantages of this modality compared to other radiologic tests. [10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-10) , [11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-11) Bedsides, ultrasound is non-invasive, exposes the patient to no radiation or contrast agents, and may lead to faster diagnoses and improved outcomes. [12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-12) However, these benefits are dependent on the proficiency and knowledge of the sonographer. [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-13) Mistakes can lead to missed diagnoses, delayed treatment, and worsened outcomes. [14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-14) - [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-17) Given this complicated dynamic, maximizing the benefits of POCUS while minimizing its risks necessitates that ED personnel use bedside ultrasound with a high degree of clinical proficiency. In 2001, the American College of Emergency Medicine (ACEP) published guidelines on the scope of POCUS in the ED that listed seven core competencies: trauma, pregnancy, abdominal aorta, cardiac, biliary, urinary tract, and procedural. [18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-18) Seven years later, they added four additional competencies: thoracic, deep vein thrombosis, ocular, and soft tissue/musculoskeletal disease. [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-19) Emergency medicine (EM) residents in the United States are required to achieve a minimum number of hours performing ultrasounds, receive didactic education, and accurately interpret a minimum number of ultrasound studies on their own. [20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-20) A recent investigation studying emergency ultrasound usage across five U.S. EM residency programs from 2013 to 2022 found that the total number of ultrasound exams conducted by each resident increased by almost 50%. [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-21) It also found that certain types of exams (such as focused assessment with sonography in trauma \[FAST\] or cardiac scans) demonstrated even more remarkable increases, while other applications (like testicular or bowel scans) remained relatively rare despite the overall surge. Other staff in the ED, such as physician assistants (PAs), nurse practitioners (NPs), and registered nurses (RNs) use POCUS in their workflows, although there is greater variability in their depth and method of ultrasound training and education. [22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-22) - [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-25) ED nurses commonly use POCUS for peripheral intravenous (IV) access in “difficult stick” patients. According to a 2022 scoping review, nurses who utilized POCUS in such cases achieved a high first-attempt success rate. [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-26) However, the review also highlighted a concern regarding the data quality due to the considerable variation and insufficient characterization of nursing training and experience with ultrasound in the existing literature. The overall extent of POCUS use in the ED by all clinical staff, its most commonly used applications, and the barriers to more frequent and efficient use have not been fully described. A better understanding of the issues that impede POCUS use, the training gaps, and the applications that staff feel most and least confident performing, may be a starting point to address barriers to its appropriate use in the ED. The purpose of this study was to characterize POCUS perceptions and utility among healthcare professionals in ED. To address this aim, a survey was sent to ED physicians, PAs, NPs, and RNs. The primary goal was to better understand their degree of training, confidence, interest level, barriers, and applications related to emergency POCUS utilization. Much of the existing literature on POCUS use in the ED focuses exclusively on emergency physicians. This study adds to the body of knowledge by incorporating viewpoints and opinions from different healthcare professionals. ## Methods ### Study Design and Survey Development This was a multi-center, cross-sectional survey study. The survey questionnaire development process aimed to capture comprehensive insights into the perceptions and applications of POCUS among healthcare professionals in the ED. Initially, an extensive literature search was conducted to identify existing questionnaires and guidelines related to POCUS in EM, drawing from reputable sources and leading authorities in the field. This literature review provided valuable insights and identified key aspects to be addressed in our study. Study investigators then collaboratively drafted an initial questionnaire tool that would discern relevant data on the level of exposure, perceptions, interest levels, and barriers to performing POCUS in the ED among physicians, PAs, NPs, and RNs. The draft underwent critical evaluation and pilot testing, with feedback received from healthcare professionals representing each professional group. To ensure methodological rigor, the survey was also reviewed by statisticians from Baylor Scott & White Research Institute. Their expertise in survey design and data analysis contributed to the optimization of the questionnaire’s structure and the appropriate selection of question formats. Iterative revisions were made to enhance the questionnaire’s clarity, validity, and reliability. The final product included 20 questions to assess the level of exposure, perceptions, interest levels, and current barriers to performing POCUS in the ED. The survey included single-answer choice questions, questions on a five-point Likert scale (strongly agree, agree, neutral, disagree, and strongly disagree), and rank-order questions where the respondent was asked to select three choices from a list of up to 13 options (e.g., “Please select the top 3 barriers for you to perform bedside ultrasound \[POCUS\] in the ED”). There was no direct funding for this study. We followed the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) reporting guidelines for cross-sectional studies. [27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-27) The survey questions were organized into four topical domains. Domain 1 inquired about employee and training information. The five questions in this domain collected information on: (1) the hospital where the respondent worked; (2) their role (physician, PA, NP, or RN); (3) the number of ultrasound machines in their department; (4) how many ultrasounds they have performed in the last month; and (5) how they were taught to perform ultrasonography (selecting the single answer choice that best describes their training method). Domain 2 asked about the perceived benefits of POCUS in the ED. There were seven questions in this domain, using a Likert scale with statements such as “Using bedside ultrasound (POCUS) allows me to rationalize ordering detailed radiological investigations and save my patient’s time and money” or “Using bedside ultrasound (POCUS) in ED improves patient/family satisfaction”. Domain 3 assessed how POCUS is utilized in the ED, and consisted of five questions in which the respondent was asked to select the top 3 scenarios that answered the question, out of thirteen answer choices. Examples of questions from this domain include ranking the POCUS applications that are most useful for diagnosis/treatment or for patient disposition. Domain 4 focused on barriers to ultrasound usage and included questions such as, “Please select the top 3 POCUS exams that you are least confident performing”, “What are the top 3 barriers for you to perform POCUS in the ED”, and “What model do you think bedside POCUS should take for best efficiency in your department”. The full survey is available in the supplemental appendix. ### Participants, Setting, and Survey Distribution Eligible study participants were physicians, PAs, NPs, and RNs who worked in an ED at one of the twelve study hospitals affiliated with Baylor Scott & White Health System and John Peter Smith Hospital in North Texas. Of the twelve total hospitals included, at the time of the survey distribution, ten were community hospitals and two were academic hospitals. Both academic hospitals have ultrasound fellowship program. The hospitals included two level-1 trauma centers and two level-3 trauma centers. Eligible participants were invited to complete the survey via email and had two weeks to do so. Invitations were sent to RNs on 8/19/2019 and closed on 9/03/2019. Physicians, PAs, and NPs were then invited to complete the survey from 9/3/2019 through 9/20/2019. Survey responses were de-identified. Non-responders were defined as those who completed less than 75% of the questions, did not submit their results, or declined informed consent. ### Data Analysis Categorical variables are reported as percentages (%). Independent t-tests, Chi-square tests or analysis of variance were conducted to test for significant differences between groups. Rank-order questions in which the respondent was asked to pick their top three answer choices are presented as counts. The study data was collected in Microsoft Excel (Microsoft Excel 2010; Microsoft Corporation, Seattle, WA) and analyzed using SPSS statistical software, version 19.0 (SPSS Inc. Chicago, IL, USA). The _p_ -value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. ## Results ### Respondent Characteristics The survey was sent to 805 total ED workers. RNs received 522 surveys and 283 surveys were sent to employees who were physicians, PAs, or NPs. Of all eligible participants, a total of 130 completed the survey (16.1% response rate). The response rate was lower for RNs (5.17%) compared to the group of other employees (36.3%). Respondent roles were as follows: 70 attending physicians, 21 PAs, 12 NPs, and 27 RNs ( [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#Table-1) ). These responses were comprised of participants working in the ED of 12 hospitals. Over half of the respondents (54.3%, n = 69) reported having performed between 1 and 10 bedside ultrasounds in the last 30 days, with more than a quarter of respondents (26.8%) having performed zero. POCUS training methods varied widely among respondents, with 55% of RNs having received no training, Physicians overall had more exposure and training with POCUS compared to non-physicians ( _p_ < 0.001). #### Table 1 . A majority (79.2%, n = 103) agreed or strongly agreed with the statement “Using bedside ultrasound (POCUS) facilitates patient disposition”. Furthermore, 92 respondents (n = 70.8%) agreed or strongly agreed with the statement “Using bedside ultrasound (POCUS) makes my diagnoses more accurate due to my ability to confirm physical findings during an exam”. Most participants agreed (70% or more) agreed that POCUS saves patient’s time and money while improving patient’s satisfaction. #### Fig. 1 . Respondent perceptions of possible benefits of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in the emergency department (ED). [![ Fig. 1 ](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/ee9b/11163417/b0d02af967a0/jacme-14-2-03-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11163417_jacme-14-2-03-g001.jpg) [Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/figure/Fig-1/) The responses to the six rank-order questions in which participants were asked to select their top three choices among a list of 13 options are presented in [Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#Table-2) . Both physicians and non-physicians agreed that FAST exams (76%) and IV access (75%) were the most useful POCUS techniques. Musculoskeletal/skin infections and abscesses, abdominal exams, and cardiac/hemodynamic assessment were also common indications for POCUS. Diagnostically, physicians tend to find cardiac assessments more useful than non-physicians (47% vs. 23% respectively, _p_ = 0.01). On the other hand, non-physicians found soft tissue/abscess assessments more useful. #### Table 2 . tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/table/Table-2/) There were differences between physicians and non-physicians for the POCUS applications that respondents felt most and least confident performing. Physicians were most confident using POCUS for FAST exams and least confident for nerve blocks. Both PAs and NPs were most confident using POCUS for musculoskeletal/skin infections and abscesses. Respondents were most interested in learning more about POCUS for FAST exams, nerve blocks, abdominal exams, and pelvic/pregnancy exams. Barriers to performing POCUS in the ED varied by respondent role ( [Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#Fig-2) ). Physicians indicated their largest barrier was that POCUS is too time-consuming. They were also concerned about the negative impact on their efficiency and workflow, and the lack of reimbursement for POCUS. The biggest obstacle to POCUS use for non-physicians was insufficient training, with over a third of participants from each non-physician employee group identifying that as their top barrier. Respondents preferred small portable ultrasound machines that can be pushed to the patient’s bedside when needed over pocket portable machines or bigger machines that stay in a procedure room where a patient would need to be taken to the machine. #### Fig. 2 . The largest barrier to point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) usage in the emergency department for emergency physicians, physician assistants, nurse practitioners, and registered nurse. [![ Fig. 2 ](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/ee9b/11163417/9f5654137e0e/jacme-14-2-03-g002.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11163417_jacme-14-2-03-g002.jpg) [Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/figure/Fig-2/) \* indicates statistically significant significance between physicians and non-physicians with p-value < 0.05. ## Discussion In this study, ED physicians, PAs, NPs, and RNs were surveyed to assess their opinions and beliefs on the perceived benefits and barriers to the use of POCUS. Their training information and the most common clinical applications were also evaluated. While there has been extensive research on similar topics, to our knowledge, this is the first study that simultaneously surveyed different types of ED employees in the same population using the same data collection tool. This offers a unique perspective and provides rich data on multiple facets that may improve POCUS usage and ultimately lead to improved patient satisfaction, financial performance, overall department efficiency, and most importantly, clinical outcomes. Emergency ultrasound offers many advantages for patients: it has multiple applications, costs less than other radiologic tests, is noninvasive, requires no contrast dye or radiation, decreases ED length of stay, requires no special preparation, may be more comfortable for patients who dislike the confined space of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or CT scan, and improves outcomes. [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-28) - [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-32) It also provides many benefits for ED staff: it is easily accessible, can be used for both diagnostic and procedural applications, increases diagnostic accuracy, decreases disposition time, hastens time to operative care, does not require consultation by other services (e.g., radiology, cardiology, obstetrics, etc.) and lowers morbidity and mortality. [33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-33) - [36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-36) Furthermore, when used properly, emergency ultrasonography increases patient satisfaction and reduces their cost of care. [37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-37) , [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-38) Despite its myriad assets, POCUS may be under-utilized in the ED when staff lack sufficient training or confidence, or face technical barriers to ultrasound implementation. The results of our study showed that the most common applications of emergency ultrasound included the FAST exam, obtaining IV access, assessing musculoskeletal skin infections, examining the abdomen and pelvis, assessing bladder volume, assessing hemodynamic stability, and for procedural guidance and nerve blocks. It is unclear if the low response rates for these applications are a result of low confidence, lack of training, or the existence of better modalities to diagnose and manage these conditions. Further research is warranted to address these barriers which may lead to an improvement in ultrasound applications for these use cases. Our findings are consistent with other recent studies on the frequency of emergency POCUS exams which report high utilization of cardiac and FAST exams and rare usage of POCUS for ocular or DVT exams. [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-21) , [39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-39) Surprisingly, our respondents indicated that musculoskeletal exams were frequently used, which stands in contrast to their relatively scarce occurrence in the studies cited. Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME) requirements mandate EM residents to complete a minimum of 150 POCUS exams to fulfill residency requirements, and many residents graduate with over double the required number. [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-21) , [40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-40) However, there is currently no standardized curriculum, prescribed number of POCUS exams, or defined proficiency criteria for NPs, PAs, or RNs in terms of graduation or credentialing related to POCUS. This was reflected in our study results, which found that there was large variation in the self-reported training methods for POCUS between physicians and other employees. Physicians’ top training method was by formal curriculum in professional school or residency. This reflects ongoing requirements for EM residencies that require ultrasound proficiency for graduation. The majority of PAs and NPs learned POCUS by bedside instruction during shifts. In the recent literature, there is a notable trend towards establishing formal ultrasound requirements for NPs and PAs practicing in the ED through the development of standardized training curricula and the definition of minimum numbers or hours of scans necessary for ultrasound credentialing in these healthcare roles. [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-25) , [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-41) Furthermore, over half of the RNs in our sample indicated that they had not received training on how to use bedside ultrasound. While this finding highlights a potential area for improvement, it is essential to acknowledge that certain ultrasound applications performed by nurses may involve lower risk profiles compared to those performed by physicians. Developing tailored and structured training programs, interdisciplinary collaboration, and adherence to best practices can contribute to safe and effective ultrasound utilization among RNs in clinical practice. Similarly, the largest barriers for POCUS usage varied among healthcare worker types. PAs, NPs, and RNs indicated that their largest barrier was insufficient training. Physicians indicated that their biggest barriers were the negative impact on their efficiency and workflow and that POCUS was time-consuming. Other variables that had been identified as barriers in previous studies were less of a concern in this sample, including poor reimbursement for bedside ultrasound exam, lack of space, resistance from other departments (radiology, surgery, etc.), unfamiliarity in documentation processes, or that an ultrasound machine/probe is not available. [42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-42) - [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-44) Our study did not specifically inquire about the use of handheld ultrasound machines, as these devices were not commonly available at the sampled hospitals during data collection. Nevertheless, it is important to recognize the significant potential of handheld ultrasound devices in overcoming barriers to POCUS utilization. Their portability, lower cost, and ease of use offer distinct advantages in the ED, addressing space constraints and enhancing the feasibility of POCUS usage, potentially leading to improved patient care and diagnostic efficiency at the bedside. [45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-45) - [48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-48) However, it is crucial to also acknowledge the challenges associated with handheld ultrasound, including billing and documentation difficulties, image quality, data management, Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) compliance, infection control, misplacement or theft, limited battery life, and other concerns. [45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-45) - [48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11163417/#ref-48) To gain a comprehensive understanding of the impact of handheld ultrasound on emergency medical practice, future research should explore healthcare professionals’ opinions, utilization patterns, and perceived advantages and disadvantages of these devices in the ED setting. Such research efforts will contribute to a more informed and evidence-based approach to implementing handheld ultrasound as a valuable tool in the ED. This study had meaningful strengths. Unlike other studies of ultrasound usage, ours included not only physicians, but also PAs, NPs, and RNs. Furthermore, it used the same standardized set of questions for all employee types to maximize comparability. Additionally, survey respondents represented two different hospital systems and 12 hospitals, which constituted trauma centers levels I (comprehensive), II (major), III (advanced), and IV (basic). These hospitals were also in a variety of geographic locations, including urban, suburban, and rural. Moreover, our study sample included both community EDs and EDs with an affiliated residency program. As such, even though the study population was limited to one state, the diversity of employee types, trauma levels, and hospital settings increases the external validity of our results. Finally, our conclusions can be used to inform the development of targeted interventions to improve POCUS use in the ED and can also be used to compare and contrast the experiences and beliefs of different types of ED workers, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of the benefits and barriers to POCUS usage. ## Limitations Despite its strengths, this study also had important limitations. Survey studies are vulnerable to both selection and reporting biases, and this work is no exception. The response rate and sample size were low, and RNs had a disproportionately lower response rate than the other employee types. Nurse response rate was potentially influenced by their demanding work schedules and patient care responsibilities. This low response rate may have introduced selection bias and restricted the generalizability of RN perspectives on POCUS usage in the ED. Future research endeavors may need to focus on improving response rates among RNs or exploring alternative data collection methods to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of their perceptions and practices regarding POCUS. However, while the response rate was low, the results of this study are similar to previous findings in the literature, suggesting that the findings may still be valid. Nevertheless, future research could benefit from using a similar study design at multiple hospitals in different states to increase both the power and validity of the results. Additionally, our study was restricted to ED personnel exclusively from North Texas. Although our respondents encompass a diverse range of backgrounds, including professions, academic and community hospitals, varying trauma center levels, and rural, suburban, and urban locations, we recognize that regional differences in training, education, and occupational models may exist in other geographic areas, thereby limiting the generalizability of our findings. Consequently, future research efforts should investigate potential regional variations and their implications for POCUS utilization in the ED. Furthermore, the results of this study may be limited by using a convenience sample, a group that may have self-selected for enthusiasm regarding the use of ultrasound. Next, this study was performed shortly before the beginning of the Covid-19 pandemic. It is difficult to determine how the pandemic has influenced opinions and utilizations of POCUS by ED staff. Understanding changes in the patterns of ultrasound use and perceptions before, during, and after the Covid-19 pandemic is a worthwhile topic of study and may further inform ultrasound training for EDs, EM residency programs, and the curricula of medical schools, PA programs, NP programs, and undergraduate nursing schools. Lastly, the survey may be limited by recall bias due to self-reporting, which is an inherent limitation to any survey study. However, while recall bias would impact questions regarding the number of ultrasounds performed or the most commonly used applications, it would not impact the responses for how ED staff learned ultrasonography or their opinions of the perceived benefits and barriers to ultrasound use. Therefore, it is reasonable that the overall effect of recall bias on our study results was minimal. In conclusion, there is a positive perception to POCUS use. Healthcare professionals have significant variation in level of training and preference of POCUS exam. POCUS is mainly used for FAST exams, IV access, musculoskeletal/skin infections and abscesses, bladder volume assessment, and cardiac/hemodynamic stability assessment. Increased POCUS education and ultrasound availability may decrease the barriers. ## Conflicts of Interest The authors declare no conflict of interest. ## Appendix - Full survey ### Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in the Emergency Department Domain 1: Employee and training information - 5 questions Domain 2: Perceived benefits - 7 questions Domain 3: Utilization - 5 questions Domain 4: Barriers to use - 3 questions Total questions: 20 ### Domain 1: Employee and training information 1\. # How Point of Care Ultrasound and Radiology Work Together

Jul 12, 2023 . 5 min read

![Venue POCUS](https://images3.cmp.optimizely.com/Zz1lZDljZjBiNjFiNjgxMWVlOThjMGUyZmVjOWU0NjYxMg==/20300_GE13839.jpg)

#### The use of point of care ultrasound and radiology ultrasound could be thought of as competing medical specialties. However, both of these imaging techniques have found their own place in medicine.

#### Ultrasound has a long history, but equipment restraints meant that it was only performed in hospitals' radiology departments until about 30 years ago. Once ultrasound machines became [smaller, more portable, and cheaper](https://www.gehealthcare.com/products/ultrasound/venue-family), the use of POCUS took off, especially in the emergency department (ED) and critical care units.1 The introduction of POCUS has expanded the role of ultrasound at the bedside, providing numerous advantages for physicians, patients, and hospital systems.

### Point of Care Ultrasound and Radiology: Workflow

To understand the advantages of POCUS, consider the workflows for both point of care ultrasound and radiology. POCUS is a bedside exam performed by the immediate supervising physician and read as the scan is being performed by that same physician. POCUS can be performed almost immediately.

##### Instead, radiology ultrasound requires the following sequence of steps:

- > The order is placed for a radiology ultrasound exam.

- > Typically, there is a wait time for radiology.

- > The patient is then transported to radiology, where a sonographer performs a scan.

- > The patient is then transported back to the original hospital location.

- > The radiologist reads the ultrasound scan and puts the report into the electronic medical record system.


Although this series of steps seems straightforward, this list doesn't factor in how busy radiology departments tend to be. Radiology exams are in high demand, leading to long wait times. By utilizing non-radiology physicians to perform POCUS exams, radiologists are free to perform the exams that require their expertise.3

##### The POCUS workflow introduces several efficiencies:

- > POCUS speeds up the scan and time to assessment.

- > The ultrasound is performed at the bedside. The patient avoids transport, and the bedside physician can incorporate POCUS findings immediately into their history and physical exam (HPI) findings—quality, severity, associated signs and symptoms, and duration. In turn, they can integrate these findings into their clinical decision-making.

- > It reduces the need to involve a second physician (the radiologist) in the patient's care, giving the radiology department more time to perform other specialized tests and exams.3


POCUS and radiology ultrasound may be similar in technique, but they are used very differently. POCUS helps answer a specific clinical question, such as "Does this patient with abdominal pain have gallstones?" It is a limited exam with a focus on answering just one clinical concern.Based on the results, the clinician can initiate different clinical pathways, such as admission and surgery consult for gallstones or signs of acute cholecystits versus further imaging if gallstones are not present.

Radiology ultrasound usually involves a more comprehensive exam, expanding the number and kind of clinical questions that physicians can answer for right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain. For example, in addition to scanning for gallstones, an abdominal ultrasound exam can evaluate for liver and pancreatic lesions if gallstones are absent, as these can both also cause RUQ pain.

To further explore point of care ultrasound and the radiology ultrasound exam, consider a hypothetical clinical scenario.

### Case Study: Point of Care Ultrasound and Radiology

It is 7 p.m. on a Saturday, and a 36 year-old woman [comes to the ED](https://www.gehealthcare.com/products/ultrasound/venue-family/emergency-care "POCUS in the ED") complaining of abdominal pain. On HPI, the patient states that she has been having right-sided abdominal pain for a few days that has become persistent and unrelenting over the past few hours. The patient also complains of nausea and lack of appetite. Upon exam, it is determined that she has pain in her RUQ. The differential diagnosis for this patient is broad, but the ER physician suspects gallbladder disease based on HPI and physical exam and puts in an order for lab tests, an ultrasound, and a CT scan.-

The lab results come back showing leukocytosis, bilirubin of 2 mg/dL, and normal lipase levels. After the lab results return, the physician has an even stronger suspicion that the patient has acute cholecystitis.

Since radiology is busy, it will be hours before the patient can get an ultrasound or CT scan. This is not unusual in many hospitals. One of the most common reasons for slow ED turnaround time is waiting for imaging—an average turnaround time for a CT scan is 5.9 hours.5

The physician instead decides to perform a POCUS exam of the patient's abdomen. If it indicates acute cholecystitis, the physician can consult surgery immediately. If the scan is negative, the doctor can schedule a CT scan.

Within 10 minutes, the ED doctor in our example scans the patient's abdomen at the bedside2, and finds gallstones and gallbladder thickening, thereby confirming the clinical suspicion that leads to a surgery consult. Additionally, the use of POCUS has allowed the patient to avoid a CT scan and exposure to radiation.

### Detecting Gallbladder Disease

Twenty million patients are affected by gallbladder disease in the US every year.Unfortunately, gallbladder disease can't be accurately diagnosed based on HPI alone, necessitating that patients undergo further imaging for a definitive diagnosis. Ultrasound can often provide helpful information in evaluating quickly for gallbladder disease.4

Traditionally, biliary ultrasound has required a trip to radiology. However, recent studies have shown that POCUS in the ED has the potential to accelerate diagnosis and treatment in patients with gallbladder disease.2 In fact, a meta-analysis showed that POCUS surpassed all other bedside modalities in sensitivity while diagnosing of biliary disease and that it is similarly accurate to the findings from radiology-diagnosed biliary disease.2

Despite the fact that biliary POCUS is both as sensitive and specific as radiology ultrasound, many surgeons want confirmatory biliary radiology ultrasound, even if POCUS shows cholelithiasis or acute cholecystitis.2 Hilsden, et al., has confirmed that the presence of gallstones found on a POCUS scan in a patient with a significant suspicion for biliary pathology is highly predictive of the eventual need for a cholecystectomy.2

Since time to diagnosis and discharge is intimately associated with ED wait times, POCUS can introduce broader efficiencies while imaging suspected biliary disease. There is a significant difference in the length of the ED stay between patients who undergo biliary POCUS and those who are referred for radiology ultrasound. Patients who underwent radiology ultrasound were in the ED for an average of 433 minutes (± 50 minutes). In contrast, POCUS patients had an average discharge time of 309 minutes (±30 minutes) after admission.2 This difference may help alleviate delays in care and ED crowding.

The POCUS exam and radiology ultrasound exam are undeniably similar in performance. However, rather than create turf wars over who carries out the exams, POCUS can help streamline clinical processes for everyone. By [assisting in diagnosis](https://www.gehealthcare.com/article/hocus-pocus-revealing-the-magic-of-ultrasound) and decreasing wait times, POCUS frees up radiologists to perform exams on cases that may be more clinically complex and require a more comprehensive exam.

# How Different Specialties Use Ultrasound, Imaging, and Procedures Daily

January 5, 2026

19 minute read

[ultrasound](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=ultrasound) [pocus](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=pocus) [imaging](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=imaging) [procedures](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=procedures) [emergency medicine](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=emergency%20medicine) [internal medicine](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=internal%20medicine) [radiology](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=radiology) [cardiology](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/search?q=cardiology)

![Medical team using point-of-care ultrasound at bedside -  for How Different Specialties Use Ultrasound, Imaging, and Procedur](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_v1_rewrite/v1_MEDICAL_SCHOOL_LIFE_AND_EXAMS_CHOOSING_A_SPECIALTY_passion_practice_choose_ideal-step2-medical-students-on-clinical-rotations-e-1993.png)

**Most medical students completely misunderstand how much ultrasound and procedures actually define day-to-day life in different specialties.**

[You are not choosing “medicine vs surgery.”](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/choosing-medical-specialty/how-program-directors-quietly-label-you-surgeon-vs-cognitive-doc) You are choosing:

- How often you live with a probe in your hand
- How often your primary tool is a CT/MRI read
- How often you’re physically doing something to a body versus thinking and talking about it

Let me break this down specialty by specialty, the way residents talk about it at 2 a.m. in the workroom, not how glossy brochures describe it.

* * *

## Big Picture: Where Imaging and Procedures Dominate

Before we dive into each field, here is the basic landscape you are choosing among:

Imaging and Procedure Intensity by Specialty

| Specialty | Ultrasound Use | Other Imaging Reliance | Procedural Intensity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Emergency Med | High | High | High |
| Internal Med | Low–Moderate | High | Low–Moderate |
| Cardiology | Very High | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| General Surgery | Moderate | Moderate | Very High |
| Radiology | Moderate | Very High | Moderate–High |
| Anesthesiology | High | Low–Moderate | High |
| OB/GYN | High | Low–Moderate | High |
| Neurology | Low | High | Low–Moderate |

You should be asking yourself three questions as you read:

1. Do I want hands on probe/needle every day, or mostly cognitive work?
2. Do I want to generate images (scan), interpret images, or both?
3. Do I want urgent, immediate feedback or slow, longitudinal decision-making?

Let us go specialty by specialty.

* * *

## Emergency Medicine: Ultrasound as a Stethoscope, Procedures as Reflex

If you like toys, chaos, and sticking needles into things at 3 a.m., emergency medicine is the benchmark.

Daily reality in a busy ED:

- You perform point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) constantly.
- You order and interpret tons of CTs, X-rays, and sometimes MRIs.
- You do procedures across almost every body system.

### How EM Uses Ultrasound Daily

This is the field where ultrasound is basically part of vital signs.

Typical EM POCUS uses per shift:

- RUQ ultrasound for gallbladder in RUQ pain
- Renal ultrasound for hydronephrosis in flank pain
- FAST/eFAST exam for trauma (RUQ, LUQ, pelvis, +/- lungs)
- Cardiac ultrasound for hypotension, dyspnea, chest pain
- Lung ultrasound for CHF vs COPD vs pneumonia vs pneumothorax
- First-trimester pregnancy scans for IUP vs ectopic concern
- Vascular ultrasound for difficult IVs and central lines

![bar chart: Trauma FAST, Cardiac, RUQ/Renal, Lung, Procedural Guidance](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_svg/chart-approximate-pocus-exams-per-busy-em-shift-9483.svg)

| Category | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Trauma FAST | 5 |
| Cardiac | 6 |
| RUQ/Renal | 4 |
| Lung | 5 |
| Procedural Guidance | 7 |

Approximate POCUS Exams per Busy EM Shift

You are not just “ordering” ultrasound. You are performing it at the bedside and making immediate decisions: Is there tamponade? Is this a wet lung? Is that gallbladder wall thickened enough that surgery needs to see this tonight?

Formal radiology studies support you, but a lot of early decisions happen based on your view of the screen.

### How EM Uses Other Imaging

EM is glued to the CT scanner. Daily:

- CT head for headache, trauma, neuro changes
- CT chest PE protocol for shortness of breath + risk factors
- CT abdomen/pelvis for undifferentiated abdominal pain
- Plain X-rays for fractures, pneumonia, bowel obstruction

You will get very good at:

- Identifying intracranial bleeds, midline shifts on CT
- Spotting obvious free air, obstruction patterns
- Reading X-rays well enough before radiology has a chance

You are not a radiologist. But the ED cannot wait 45 minutes for a formal read on a crashing patient.

### Procedures in EM

This is a procedure-heavy field, especially in training. Daily or near-daily:

- Laceration repairs (simple to complex)
- Incision and drainage of abscesses
- Peripheral IVs, external jugular lines, ultrasound-guided IVs
- Splinting and basic fracture care
- Bedside reductions (shoulder, elbow, hip in some settings)

Less frequent but common in many EDs:

- Central venous lines (often ultrasound-guided)
- Lumbar punctures
- Intubations and airway management
- Chest tubes and pigtails
- Procedural sedations for reductions, cardioversions
- Paracentesis, thoracentesis in ED boarding patients

If you want daily mixture: talk–scan–cut–stitch–interpret, EM is close to ideal.

* * *

## Internal Medicine and Hospitalist Work: Imaging-Heavy, Procedures Optional

Most internal medicine (IM) residents discover some harsh truth early: the culture of procedures varies wildly by institution.

At many academic centers:

- Ultrasound: growing but still underused by attendings; residents use it more.
- Procedures: often offloaded to IR, anesthesia, or procedure teams.
- Imaging: absolutely central to patient care.

### Ultrasound in Internal Medicine

There is a huge spread here.

At a “classic” academic IM department from 10 years ago:

- Almost no POCUS by attendings.
- Residents occasionally use portable machines for IVC/volume status.

Modern trend (and where the field is going):

- Focused cardiac ultrasound for LV function, effusion, gross RV strain.
- Lung ultrasound for B-lines, pleural effusions, pneumothorax.
- Abdominal scans for ascites pockets for paracentesis.
- Vascular guidance for difficult IVs or lines when procedure team unavailable.

![Mermaid timeline diagram](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_svg/mermaid-growth-of-pocus-in-internal-medicine-training-6532.svg)

| Period | Event |
| --- | --- |
| 2005 | Rare, mostly ICU-focused |
| 2012 | IM interest groups, a few champions |
| 2018 | Formal POCUS curricula in many residencies |
| 2024 | Expectation at many programs, board questions |

Growth of POCUS in Internal Medicine Training

If you want ultrasound to be a core identity, IM alone will probably frustrate you unless you are in a POCUS-heavy residency or go into a subspecialty like critical care or cardiology.

### Imaging in Internal Medicine

This is where IM is heavy.

You live in:

- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for most undifferentiated problems
- MRI brain/spine for neuro complaints that land on your service
- Echocardiography (read by cardiology but heavily used)
- Nuclear imaging (V/Q scans, stress tests)
- Daily chest X-rays in ICUs

You need to be very comfortable correlating image findings with clinical reality. Example: patient with “CT showing possible pneumonia vs atelectasis” – you decide if they get antibiotics.

### Procedures: Highly Program-Dependent

Common on a procedure-heavy IM service or ICU rotation:

- Paracentesis
- Thoracentesis
- Central venous catheter placement
- Arterial line placement
- Lumbar puncture
- Bedside temporary dialysis catheters in some centers

On a procedure-lite hospitalist service:

- You do almost none of these.
- Everything goes to IR, anesthesia, or a dedicated procedure team.

So IM is imaging-heavy, ultrasound-moderate (and growing), procedures-optional depending heavily on setting.

## Cardiology: Living in Echo, Cath Labs, and Devices

Cardiology is one of the clearest examples of a field where imaging and procedures define your week.

### Ultrasound in Cardiology

Echo is the backbone of modern cardiology.

Daily:

- You interpret transthoracic echos (TTE) and possibly transesophageal (TEE).
- You look at function (EF), valves, wall motion, pericardial effusions.
- You perform focused bedside echo in CCU/ICU settings.

Cardiology fellows and attendings:

- Often sit at echo reading stations for hours.
- Use echo to guide nearly every major decision: valve interventions, cardiomyopathy management, shock states.

If you enjoy the visual logic of: “abnormal wall motion here, consider LAD lesion,” you will probably like this part.

### Other Imaging in Cardiology

Heavy use of:

- Coronary angiography (invasive imaging, cath lab)
- Cardiac CT for coronary anatomy, TAVR planning
- Cardiac MRI for cardiomyopathies, myocarditis, viability, infiltrative disease
- Nuclear stress imaging and PET perfusion studies

![doughnut chart: Echo, Cath/Angio, Stress/Nuclear, CT/MRI](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_svg/chart-typical-imaging-mix-in-a-general-cardiology-practi-9432.svg)

| Category | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Echo | 50 |
| Cath/Angio | 25 |
| Stress/Nuclear | 15 |
| CT/MRI | 10 |

Typical Imaging Mix in a General Cardiology Practice

The exact numbers vary, but echo dominates.

### Procedures in Cardiology

If you choose interventional or EP (electrophysiology), you will live in procedure land.

Interventional:

- Coronary angiograms and PCI (stents)
- Structural work: TAVR, MitraClip, ASD/PFO closures
- Some mechanical support device placements

EP:

- Cardiac ablations (AFib, SVT, VT)
- Device implantation: pacemakers, ICDs, CRT devices
- Device revisions and extractions

Even non-interventional cardiologists will:

- Perform TEEs
- Do cardioversions
- Place temporary pacing wires occasionally

If you want a tight marriage of imaging + invasive work with high acuity, cardiology is a strong candidate.

* * *

## Radiology: Pure Imaging, Procedures If You Want Them

Radiology is the obvious imaging kingdom, but there is nuance in how procedures figure into daily life.

### Core Radiology: Reading, Not Scanning

As a diagnostic radiologist:

- You interpret studies, you do not generally operate the machines.
- Ultrasound techs, CT/MRI techs acquire images, you read them.

Daily stack:

- CT of almost every body part
- MRI brain/spine/joints/liver/heart depending on subspecialty
- Ultrasound (abdomen, OB, vascular, thyroid, scrotal)
- Plain films (LOTS of chest X-rays)
- Nuclear medicine (bone scans, PET/CT)

Ultrasound is a big part of your day but as an interpreter. Not with a probe in your own hand most of the time.

### Interventional Radiology (IR): The Procedure-Heavy Side

If you are procedure-obsessed but also love imaging, IR is extremely attractive.

Daily IR practice:

- Image-guided biopsies (liver, lung, lymph node, bone)
- Central venous catheters, ports, PICCs
- Drainage procedures (abscess, biliary, nephrostomy, pleural)
- Vascular interventions (angioplasty, stents, embolizations)
- Tumor ablations (RFA, microwave, cryo)
- Uterine fibroid embolizations, prostate artery embolizations

All of this under fluoroscopy, CT, and ultrasound guidance. You live inside imaging while doing procedures.

* * *

## Anesthesiology and Critical Care: Real-Time Ultrasound and Lines

Anesthesiology is quietly one of the most procedure-heavy specialties, and modern practice is deeply tied to ultrasound.

### Ultrasound in Anesthesia

Common daily uses:

- Ultrasound-guided peripheral nerve blocks (in regional-heavy practices, this is constant)
- Vascular access: internal jugular, subclavian, femoral central lines
- Arterial lines in difficult anatomy
- Transthoracic or transesophageal echo in cardiac anesthesia
- Lung ultrasound in ICU for fluid status, pneumothorax, effusions

If you like real-time needle visualization and sonoanatomy, anesthesia and critical care offer that every day.

### Imaging and Procedures

Imaging outside ultrasound is less central to anesthesia, but:

- You interpret basic portable chest X-rays in ICUs.
- You correlate ABGs, ventilator waveforms, and X-rays regularly.

Procedures are nonstop:

- Endotracheal intubations
- LMA placement
- Arterial lines
- Central lines
- Epidurals and spinals
- Peripheral nerve blocks
- Bronchoscopies in some practices

Critical care anesthesiologists also do:

- Percutaneous tracheostomies
- Bedside ultrasound-guided drainage (thoracentesis, paracentesis in some units)

If you want your “imaging” to be almost entirely ultrasound in-your-own-hands, anesthesia/CCM is a strong fit.

* * *

## General Surgery and Surgical Subspecialties: Imaging to Plan, Hands to Fix

Surgery lives on the other end of the spectrum: you use imaging to decide what to cut, then you cut. And you use intraoperative imaging more than you think.

### Ultrasound in Surgery

General surgery:

- Intraoperative ultrasound during hepatobiliary cases (liver resections, cholecystectomies with concern for CBD stones).
- Bedside ultrasound in trauma (FAST/eFAST), although EM often leads this.

Vascular surgery:

- Heavy duplex ultrasound use to evaluate arterial and venous disease.
- Intraoperative ultrasound to assess grafts, stents, flow.

Breast surgery:

- Ultrasound for lesion localization, biopsies, and wire placement.

Orthopedics:

- Less ultrasound, more X-ray and intraoperative fluoroscopy.
- Some sports surgeons use ultrasound in clinic for injections.

Neurosurgery:

- Limited ultrasound; heavy CT/MRI reliance.
- Intraoperative ultrasound in some tumor resections.

### Other Imaging for Surgeons

You live in the radiology report.

- CT abdomen/pelvis for appendicitis, SBO, diverticulitis, perforation.
- CT angiography for vascular pathologies.
- MRI for soft tissue, spine, joint, and brain pathologies.
- Fluoroscopy intraoperatively for ortho, spine, vascular.

Procedures? The whole job is procedures. Outpatient, inpatient, OR. Lines and drains are a side note.

### Everyday Surgical Procedures (Outside the OR)

- Bedside I&Ds
- Chest tubes
- Central lines (if not taken by ICU or anesthesia)
- J-tube/G-tube management
- Wound vac changes, bedside debridements

## OB/GYN: Ultrasound and Procedures from Clinic to OR

OB/GYN is one of the most ultrasound-heavy clinical fields, and it blends clinic procedures, L&D, and OR work.

### Ultrasound in OB/GYN

OB side:

- First-trimester viability scans
- Dating scans
- Anatomy scans (usually done by techs but interpreted by MFM/OBs)
- Bedside ultrasound on L&D: fetal position, AFI estimates, BPPs

GYN side:

- Pelvic ultrasound for fibroids, ovarian masses, IUD checks, abnormal bleeding
- In fertility practices, transvaginal ultrasound is constant.

Many OB/GYNs are capable of doing basic scanning themselves. In high-resource settings, techs do most formal scans, but bedside scans on L&D still happen frequently.

### Procedures

[Clinic procedures:](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/choosing-medical-specialty/hospital-based-vs-clinic-heavy-specialties-a-detailed-day-in-the-life-map)

- IUD insertions and removals
- Endometrial biopsies
- Colposcopy with biopsies
- Hysteroscopies (office or OR depending on practice)

L&D procedures:

- Vaginal deliveries (yes, repetitive but technically demanding)
- Operative vaginal deliveries (vacuum, forceps in some practices)
- C-sections (a major surgical procedure, often daily)

GYN surgery:

- Laparoscopic hysterectomies, oophorectomies, salpingectomies
- Hysteroscopic polypectomies, myomectomies
- Open surgery for large masses or malignancy (more in Gyn Onc)

OB/GYN is an excellent fit if you want ultrasound to be omnipresent and you enjoy a lot of hands-on procedures with a women’s health focus.

* * *

## Neurology and Neurosurgery: MRI Kingdom, Procedures Split

You already know neurology is MRI-heavy. What many students misjudge is how procedure-light regular neurology is versus how invasive neurosurgery becomes.

### Neurology

Ultrasound:

- Limited but present: carotid duplex interpretation, transcranial Doppler in some stroke centers.
- Not core to daily practice in most general neurology clinics.

Imaging:

- MRI brain/spine dominates.
- CT head for acute presentations.
- CTA/MRA for vascular imaging.

Procedures:

- Lumbar punctures (often done by neurology residents).
- EMG/NCS (electrodiagnostic studies) – technical and very hands-on but not “invasive” in a surgical sense.
- Botulinum toxin injections for movement disorders, spasticity, chronic migraine (procedural, but needles rather than scalpels).

If you want heavy imaging interpretation but minimal invasive procedures, general neurology fits.

### Neurosurgery

Different beast entirely.

- CT/MRI used constantly to plan and assess surgical interventions.
- Intraoperative neuronavigation uses these images in 3D.

Procedures: the entire specialty.

- Craniotomies, spine decompressions, fusions, tumor resections, aneurysm clippings or endovascular work (if in hybrid practices).

## Primary Care, Pediatrics, and “Low-Procedure” Fields

You will also want clarity on areas that are relatively lighter in imaging/procedures, because some of you simply do not enjoy needles and machines.

### Outpatient Adult Primary Care

Ultrasound:

- Minimal in most traditional practices.
- Growing field: primary care POCUS for MSK injections, abdominal exams, DVT checks, but still niche.

Imaging:

- You order lots of imaging, but you do not perform or interpret in depth.
- Chest X-rays for cough, CT for persistent symptoms, mammograms ordered, not read.

Procedures:

- Joint injections (knees, shoulders) in some practices.
- Skin biopsies, cryotherapy.
- Pap smears, IUDs if you choose to do women’s health.

You can make your practice more procedural-heavy, but it is optional.

### Pediatrics

Ultrasound:

- Very limited in general outpatient pediatrics.
- In pediatric emergency or PICU, POCUS is gaining ground.

Imaging:

- You interpret many X-rays at a basic level: pneumonia, fractures, constipation.
- Complex imaging read by radiology.

Procedures:

- Vaccines (yes, they count technically but not what people worry about)
- LPs in inpatient settings
- Simple I&Ds, splinting, foreign body removal in ED or urgent care.

Peds is cognitively and developmentally rich, but imaging and procedures are generally lower intensity unless you subspecialize (e.g., peds cardiology, peds IR, peds surgery).

## Matching Your Personality to Imaging and Procedure Profiles

Here is the part that matters for your specialty choice.

Ask yourself honestly:

- Do I enjoy the feeling of holding a needle / probe / scalpel and seeing immediate physical change?
- Do I enjoy staring at a screen of grayscale images and deducing a diagnosis?
- Do I want more of my day in dark rooms or bright wards?
- Do I want my “wins” to be: “the CT showed exactly what I thought,” or “I fixed this with my hands,” or “I reoriented the whole care plan with my reasoning”?

![stackedBar chart: EM, IM/Hospitalist, Cardiology, Radiology, Anesthesia, Gen Surg](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_svg/chart-procedural-vs-imaging-vs-cognitive-focus-by-field-4189.svg)

| Category | Procedural | Imaging Focus | Primarily Cognitive |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| EM | 40 | 25 | 35 |
| IM/Hospitalist | 15 | 35 | 50 |
| Cardiology | 30 | 35 | 35 |
| Radiology | 25 | 60 | 15 |
| Anesthesia | 45 | 25 | 30 |
| Gen Surg | 60 | 20 | 20 |

Procedural vs Imaging vs Cognitive Focus by Field

If you want:

- **Maximal ultrasound + procedures**: EM, anesthesia/critical care, OB/GYN, interventional radiology, some cardiology tracks.
- **Maximal imaging, minimal “blood and guts” procedures**: diagnostic radiology, neurology, non-interventional cardiology, some hospitalist roles.
- **Procedures but imaging just as a planning tool**: surgery and its subspecialties, neurosurgery, ortho.
- **Mostly cognitive with optional minor procedures**: outpatient IM, primary care, pediatrics, psychiatry.

[Your clerkships can lie to you.](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/choosing-medical-specialty/why-your-core-clerkship-comments-matter-more-than-your-stated-specialty) The IM attending who loves POCUS is not the national average. The surgeon who never touches the ultrasound machine is also not the full story. So look at patterns across institutions.

* * *

## Practical Tips for Medical Students on Rotations

A few concrete things you can do now:

1. Track what you actually enjoy doing. Make a quick note on your phone:

   - “Liked: doing FAST exam in trauma, doing paracentesis, reading CTs with senior.”
   - “Disliked: sitting through 45 minutes of unfiltered MRI reads, doing 10th Pap of the day.”
2. [Ask residents blunt questions:](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/choosing-medical-specialty/shadowing-vs-sub-i-what-faculty-actually-use-to-predict-your-fit)

   - “How many procedures did you personally do this week?”
   - “How often do you hold the ultrasound probe versus a tech?”
   - “Which imaging studies do you feel comfortable interpreting on your own?”
3. Spend elective time in radiology and ICU. They are imaging and procedure hubs that give you a clearer signal of what you like.

4. During Step and shelf studying, pay attention to what pulls you in.

   - Echo diagrams excite you? Cardiology or anesthesia.
   - CT patterns and subtle findings excite you? Radiology or EM.
   - OR anatomy and procedural steps excite you? Surgery or OB/GYN.

![Resident performing bedside ultrasound in ICU -  for How Different Specialties Use Ultrasound, Imaging, and Procedures Daily](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_v1_rewrite/v1_MEDICAL_SCHOOL_LIFE_AND_EXAMS_CHOOSING_A_SPECIALTY_pediatrics_geriatrics_comparative_analys-step1-pediatrics-vs-geriatrics-medical-special-1836.png)

* * *

## Quick Visual: Who Actually Holds the Probe?

![Mermaid flowchart LR diagram](https://cdn.residencyadvisor.com/images/articles_svg/mermaid-who-operates-ultrasound-vs-interprets-6192.svg)

Who Operates Ultrasound vs Interprets

It is very different to be the one physically moving the probe versus being the one in the dark room reading 50 ultrasounds in a row. Decide which you actually want.

* * *

## FAQs

### 1\. If I love ultrasound specifically, which specialties give me the most hands-on probe time?

Emergency medicine, anesthesiology/critical care, OB/GYN (especially on L&D), and interventional radiology give you the most consistent hands-on ultrasound use. Cardiology is ultrasound-heavy, but a lot of the scanning is done by techs; you interpret and occasionally perform focused studies. Internal medicine is catching up, especially in ICU and hospitalist POCUS-focused practices, but it is not yet universally probe-heavy.

### 2\. I like imaging a lot but do not want much blood or invasive procedures. What should I look at?

Diagnostic radiology is the clear choice if you want imaging to be your core and procedures to be limited or mostly minimally invasive. Neurology and non-interventional cardiology also rely heavily on imaging but involve relatively few invasive procedures. Outpatient IM and pediatrics will have you ordering and discussing imaging constantly but only doing minor procedures if you choose.

### 3\. Will I get enough exposure to procedures and ultrasound during core clerkships to make a good decision?

Usually not. Core clerkships often underexpose you to the real procedural volume of fields like anesthesiology, radiology/IR, and critical care. You see a skewed version of IM (floor months with few procedures) and surgery (OR but not clinic). Use electives, ICU rotations, ED shifts, and radiology rotations very deliberately during MS3–MS4 to sample the true day-to-day of imaging and procedures in those fields.

### 4\. How much do imaging and procedures matter for long-term job satisfaction?

A lot. I have seen residents in IM who thought they were “medicine people” burn out because they missed hands-on procedures they loved in EM. I have also seen surgery juniors realize they hate spending all day scrubbed in and would rather interpret CTs and guide care. Your tolerance for screens vs scalpels, probes vs paperwork, and acute procedures vs longitudinal thinking will shape how drained or energized you feel a decade from now. Treat this as a core decision variable, not a footnote.

* * *

**Key takeaways:**

1. Different specialties do not just “use imaging”; they have completely different relationships to ultrasound, CT/MRI, and procedures—decide whether you want to generate, interpret, or act on images.
2. If you want daily hands-on work with probes, needles, and devices, lean toward EM, anesthesia/critical care, OB/GYN, IR, and procedural cardiology or surgery; if you prefer cognitive and interpretive work, radiology, neurology, IM, and outpatient fields fit better.
3. [Do not rely on one rotation’s culture—use electives and honest resident conversations to calibrate how ultrasound, imaging, and procedures actually show up in the real version of each specialty.](https://residencyadvisor.com/resources/choosing-medical-specialty/the-danger-of-locking-in-a-specialty-after-one-great-rotation)
